coredata.dc:title: Novel carbon dots as effective corrosion inhibitor for N80 steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaodan
Li, Jianbo
Deng, Chaoyi
Yang, Lan
Lv, Jie
Fu, Luoping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the eco-friendly of functionalized carbon dots (N-CDs) was synthesized by the microwave method. The corrosion inhibition behavior of N-CDs for N80 steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions were systematically investigated by weight loss, electrochemical measurements and surface morphology. The results revealed that N-CDs exhibited outstanding inhibitory performance. The inhibitory efficiency in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 70 °C reached 89.04% and 94.6% at 200 mg/L of N-CDs, respectively. Meanwhile, N-CDs was a mixed inhibitor that mainly suppressed the cathode in corrosion conditions (1 M HCl), while it was a mixed inhibitor that primarily inhibited the anode in the saline solution. In addition, based on the analysis and mechanism study of the surface of N80 steel, N-CDs followed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and adsorbed on the metal surface through physical and chemical adsorption to inhibit corrosion, which fully explained the inhibition mechanism of N-CDs at metal/solution interface. Therefore, this work could provide new ideas for exploring efficient and environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Corrosion inhibitor
Inhibition properties
Microwave method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FTIR, UV–vis, and XRD analysis of N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR, UV–vis and XRD spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs (a) FTIR; (b) UV–vis; (c) XRD.
XPS spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs (a) XPS-full spectrum; (b) XPS-C 1 s; (c) XPS-N 1 s; (d) XPS-O 1 s.
TEM, grain size of N-CDs (a) TEM image; (b) grain size distribution image.
Corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency of N80 steel in various test solutions for 72 h at 70 °C (a) 1 M HCl solution; (b) CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
Corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency of N80 steel in different temperature for 72 h (a) 1 M HCl solution; (b) CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
Tafel curves of N80 steel in various test solutions (a) 1 M HCl solution; (b) CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
EIS images of N80 steel in various test environments after 72 h immersion (a) (b) (c) 1 M HCl solution; (d) (e) (f) CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
Equivalent circuit diagram (a) blank solution (b) added inhibitor concentration.

The surface analysis of N80 steel in various test solutions after 72 h immersion at 70 °C (a, b) blank-HCl solution; (c, d) blank- NaCl solution; (e, f) 200 mg/L N-CDs- HCl; (g, h) 200 mg/L N-CDs- NaCl.

The element distribution of N80 steel in various test solutions after 72 h immersion at 70 °C (a, b) blank-HCl solution; (c, d) blank-NaCl solution; (e, f) 200 mg/L N-CDs- HCl; (g, h) 200 mg/L N-CDs-NaCl.

3D and 2D corrosion morphology of N80 steel in various test solutions after 72 h immersion at 70 °C (a, b) blank-HCl solution; (c, d) blank-NaCl solution; (e, f) 200 mg/L N-CDs-HCl; (g, h) 200 mg/L N-CDs-NaCl.
Langmuir adsorption isotherms of specimen in various test solutions: (a) 1 M HCl solution; (b) CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
Schematic illustration of the inhibition mechanism for N-CDs at the surface of N80 steel in (a) 1 M HCl solution; (b) CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
Potential polarization curve parameters for N80 steel 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions.
Impedance parameters of N80 carbon steel in various test solutions.

head.ce:title: Novel carbon dots as effective corrosion inhibitor for N80 steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610500 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610500 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Industrial Environmental Monitoring Research Institute, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610500 China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bIndustrial Environmental Monitoring Research Institute, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610500 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sichuan Dongshu New Material Co., Ltd, Deyang, Sichuan, 618000 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan Dongshu New Material Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSichuan Dongshu New Material Co., Ltd, Deyang, Sichuan, 618000 China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the eco-friendly of functionalized carbon dots (N-CDs) was synthesized by the microwave method. The corrosion inhibition behavior of N-CDs for N80 steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions were systematically investigated by weight loss, electrochemical measurements and surface morphology. The results revealed that N-CDs exhibited outstanding inhibitory performance. The inhibitory efficiency in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 70 °C reached 89.04% and 94.6% at 200 mg/L of N-CDs, respectively. Meanwhile, N-CDs was a mixed inhibitor that mainly suppressed the cathode in corrosion conditions (1 M HCl), while it was a mixed inhibitor that primarily inhibited the anode in the saline solution. In addition, based on the analysis and mechanism study of the surface of N80 steel, N-CDs followed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and adsorbed on the metal surface through physical and chemical adsorption to inhibit corrosion, which fully explained the inhibition mechanism of N-CDs at metal/solution interface. Therefore, this work could provide new ideas for exploring efficient and environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:para: In the early stages of oilfield exploitation and injection-production (such as Daqing and Xinjiang oilfields in China), because the geological structure of the underground environment is very unfavorable for oil exploitation, acid injection (such as HCl, H2SO4, etc.) can loosen the rock structure and increase the flow rate of oil and gas. Conducive to later mining. In the well injection stage, CO2 flooding is often used to improve oilfield recovery. By injecting CO2 downhole, the difference between formation pressure and downhole pressure is used to produce oil and gas. In the process, CO2 dissolves to form carbonic acid, and the corrosion problem becomes more serious due to the erosion of Cl− content in the formation. However, whether it is an acid injection or CO2 flooding, it will cause serious corrosion to equipment and pipelines in the process. [1,2] Therefore, solving the two major corrosive environments (acid corrosion and CO2 corrosion) encountered in oil fields will have great research significance for the petrochemical industry.

Severe corrosion caused by the harsh environment has attracted widespread attention in recent years. Currently, the inhibitory properties of organic compounds (such as Mannich base [3,4], Schiff bases [5–7], imidazolines [8–11] and ionic liquids [12]) have been reported. However, these organic compounds are high toxicity, difficult biodegradation, and complex synthesis in the petrochemical industry, which to a certain extent have a certain impact on the environment and limit their application [13–16].

Carbon dots (CDs), as novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, have attracted plentiful attention for their good water-solubility and low cytotoxicity and are widely used in pharmaceutical, sensing, and catalytic applications [17]. Meanwhile, the application of carbon dots was discovered in the corrosion protective field and presented an excellent result [18–20]. Compared with traditional corrosion inhibitors, the raw materials for the synthesis of carbon points (carbon source and nitrogen source) are widely available, cheap, low energy consumption, and avoid the toxicity and environmental hazards from metal elements and organic compounds. Therefore, if it is applied to the petrochemical industry, its economic benefits and environmental impact will be immeasurable. Recently, Cui et al. [17] reported for the first time that N-doped CDs prepared by the hydrothermal method was used in the field of anti-corrosion and the corrosion of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution was tremendously inhibited after the addition of N-doped CDs. Ye et al. [21] synthesized a kind of N-CDs using methacrylic acid and n-butylamine, manifested that N-CDs could be used as an effective inhibitor to block carbon steel/solution contact by adsorbing on the surface of Q235 carbon steel, and the inhibition efficiency of N-CDs in 1 M HCl solution was 94.96% at 200 mg/L. Hong et al. [22] reported a facile solvothermal method to synthesize (N and S) co-doped carbon dots and applied in cell imaging detection. By consulting related literature, we can understand that people discussing the corrosion resistance of carbon dots mostly use a single corrosive medium solution for testing, and the time is mostly 24 h or even lower, and the temperature is below 50 °C. However, in saltwater or hydrochloric acid corrosive medium solution, there are few carbon dots performance studies exceeding 50 °C and 48 h [13,23–26]. In addition, there are few reports about the application of carbon dots synthesized by microwave method in the field of anti-corrosion. Inspired by the inhibitory properties of carbon dots, we anticipate that synthesized CDs will have a long-term and temperature protective effect on carbon steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions due to their special characteristics.

In this paper, an environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitor N-CDs was synthesized by the microwave method. The structure and composition of N-CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The corrosion inhibition performance of N-CDs on N80 steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions was investigated by using polarization curve (Tafel), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and weight loss test. The surface morphology of N80 carbon steel was analyzed by atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS).

In this experiment, carbon steel (N80) samples were used as experimental specimens. N80 steel used in this work was composed of (wt%) 0.24% C, 0.22% Si, 1.19% Mn, 0.013% P, 0.004% S, 0.036% Gr, 0.021% Mo, 0.028% Ni and Fe balance. Specimens with dimensions of 40 × 13 × 2 mm were used for weight loss measurements. The working electrode with an exposed 1 cm2 area was used in electrochemical experiments. Before all test, the specimens were polished and smoothed with silicon carbide sandpaper (400#, 600#, 800#, 1200#), then degreased with ethanol and acetone, and finally dried at room temperature.

Sodium salicylate (98%), tetraethylenepentamine (95.5%) and urea (99%) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. The aggressive solution was prepared by diluting hydrochloric acid (37%) and sodium chloride (≥99.5%) with deionized water, and the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution infused with saturated CO2 gas for 1 h.

The synthesis of N-CDs referenced literature Lv Jie et al. [27] The synthesis method was synthesized by the microwave method, and the preparation diagram was shown in Fig 1 . A mixture of 1.6 g sodium salicylate, 3.8 mL tetraethylenepentamine and 0.6 g urea were ultrasonically dissolved. Subsequently, these substances were transferred into a 700 W microwave oven for 270 S. After the reaction, the mixture was automatically cooled to room temperature, a small amount of deionized water was added. Then, the mixture was transferred to a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 60 min to obtain the crude product. After that, the crude product was purified by a dialysis bag (retaining molecular weight of 1000 Da) for 48 h, during which the deionized water was replaced every 3 h. At the end of dialysis, the excess solvent was removed with a rotary evaporator. Finally, the product was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the final N-CDs.

The chemical structure of N-CDs was characterized by Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (WQF520, China), XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, England), X Pert PRO MPD X-ray diffractometer (Scan range 10°–70°, PANalytical B.V., Netherlands), TEM (JEOL JEM-2100, Japan) and UV-3600 (Shimadzu, Japan).

The static weight loss test referenced to the People's Republic of China Petroleum and Natural Gas Industry Standard SY/T5405–1996. N80 steel size was 40 × 13 × 2 mm. The N80 steel specimens were immersed in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions without and with various concentrations of the N-CDs at 70 °C for 72 h. 1 M HCl was diluted with 37% HCl. The configuration method of CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions: first, 3.5 wt% NaCl solution was configured under normal pressure, then CO2 was injected into the solution until it was saturated, and then placed the solution in a 70 °C water bath for evaluation. The measured pH value range was 5.2–5.5. After that, samples were taken out, they were rinsed with deionized water, then degreased and dehydrated with acetone, ethanol, and finally dried. The experiment was calculated three times to obtain the average corrosion rate.

The corrosion rate (ν corr ) and inhibition efficiency (η) were calculated by the following equation:
(1)
v
c o r r
=
Δ
W
S × t
Where ΔW, S, t, were the average weight loss (g) of the three specimens, exposed sample area (cm2), immersion time (h), respectively.
(2)
η =
r 0
− r
r 0
× 100 %
Where r 0 and r were the corrosion rates of N80 steel in absence and presence of inhibitors, respectively.

The electrochemical measurements referred to the People's Republic of China Petroleum and Natural Gas Industry Standard SY/T5405–1996. The electrochemical workstation CHI650 and the traditional three-electrode system were used to perform electrochemical experiments at 70 ± 1 °C in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions. The three-electrode system consists of 1 cm2 N80 carbon steel as the working electrode (WE), saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode (SCE) and platinum sheet as the counter electrode (CE), which were penetrated into 250 mL aggressive solution at 70 °C. Before testing, the open circuit potential test (OCP) was stable for approximately 1 h. The scanning range of the polarization curve was open circuit potential from -250 mV to 250 mV at 1 mV/s. The electrochemical impedance measurements were performed within 100 KHz–0.01 Hz with 5 mV amplitude and the simulated parameter values were obtained by fitting with the ZsimDemo software.

The morphologies of N80 steel surface after 72 h immersion at 70 °C in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions with blank and 200 mg/L N-CDs added were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Atomic force microscope (AFM). The element distribution of the N80 steel surface was obtained by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). All specimens were rinsed with distilled water, then dried with N2 gas and stored in the vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, UV–vis, and XRD analysis of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The FTIR, UV–vis, and XRD spectra were conducted to characterize the chemical structures of N-CDs, shown in Fig. 2 . The adsorption peaks (Fig. 2(a)) at 3431, 2962, 1636, 1484, and 1378 cm−1 were ascribed to O-H, N-H, N-C=O, C-N, C-O, respectively. The peak at 2113 cm−1 was the cumulative double bond typical adsorption of C=O=N and was different from the raw material [20,28–30]. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2(b)), the only weak absorption peak at 296 nm was associated with the
n −
π *
electronic transition [24,31,32]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2(c)) showed that there was a broad diffraction peak around 2θ=25° and the prepared N-CDs was a complete amorphous structure [16,31,33].

XPS spectrum was applied to analyze the chemical compositions of N-CDs and the full XPS spectrum was presented in Fig. 3 . It could be seen from that C, N, O, and Na were the main elements of N-CDs (Fig. 3(a)), which indicated the occurrence of the microwave reaction. Correspondingly, the high-resolution XPS spectra (C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s) were exhibited in Fig. 3b-d. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(b)) demonstrated three peaks at 284.4, 285.6, and 287.7 eV, which were ascribed to groups C-C, C-O/C-N, C=O, respectively [14,21,34,35]. Meanwhile, the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) could be divided into N-H, N-C and C=N bond, located at 399.2 eV, 400.1 eV and 401.3 eV, respectively [16,36,37]. These results showed that N atoms were successfully doped into carbon dots. Furthermore, the O 1 s peak (Fig. 3(d)) at 531.0 eV and 532.3 eV were associated with C-O and C=O.

In order to better study the microscopic morphology of N-CDs, transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to analyze grain size, and the result displayed in Fig. 4 . The grain size distribution chart (Fig. 4(b)) gave the approximate size. The synthesized N-CDs was uniformly dispersed in the solution with small courtyard black spots similar to the spherical structure. The particle size range in the particle size distribution diagram was 10–24 nm and an average particle size about 16 nm, indicating good water solubility and dispersibility in distilled water [28,33,37].

The weight loss test of N80 steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 70 °C with various concentrations of N-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 . After 72 h immersion, the corrosion rates of blank hydrochloric acid and saline solution were 0.0072 g cm−2 h−1 and 0.0068 g cm−2 h−1, respectively, indicating that the N80 steel was severely corroded in the two corrosive mediums [38,39]. However, with the addition of N-CDs, the corrosion rate decreased and the inhibition efficiency increased. When the concentration of N-CDs was 200 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency in two aggressive solutions reached up to 89.04% and 94.6%, respectively. Meanwhile, the inhibition performance was strongly dependent on the concentration of N-CDs.

Fig. 6 revealed the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency for 72 h at different temperatures. It could be seen that without adding corrosion inhibitor, the corrosion rate increased sharply with the temperature, and the N80 steel was severely corroded. However, with the addition of N-CDs, the degree of corrosion was obviously inhibited, but when the temperature exceeded 70 °C, the corrosion rate after adding corrosion inhibitors also increased slightly, and the inhibition efficiency decreased slightly. This was mainly due to the increase in temperature, the reduction of the adsorption capacity of the corrosion inhibitor on the surface of the N80 carbon steel, and the increase of the desorption capacity, and the corrosion rate of the steel sheet itself would increase, which ultimately affected the inhibition effect. At a temperature of 90 °C, the inhibition efficiency of N80 steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution reached 80.5% and 85.2%, respectively, demonstrating that N-CDs had a certain temperature resistance.

The potentiodynamic polarization curves of N80 steel added with different concentrations of N-CDs after immersed in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions at 70 °C were exhibited in Fig. 7 . Electrochemical parameters obtained by extrapolation of polarization curve, anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (β a and β c), corrosion current density (I corr) and corrosion potential (Ecorr ) were shown in Table 1 . The inhibition efficiency obtained from the following equation:
(3)
η =
I
c o r r
0
−
I
c o r r
1
I
c o r r
0
× 100 %
where
I
corr
0
and
I
corr
1
were correspond to the corrosion current density values in the absence and presence of inhibitors, respectively.

Combined with Fig. 7 and Table 1, it could be seen from these curves that the corrosion current density displayed a downtrend with the increase of N-CDs dosage in the two corrosion medium solutions, suggesting that the N-CDs could act as an effective inhibitor to prevent corrosion of N80 carbon steel. From Table 1 that
E
c o r r
values were less than 85 mV between blank and the presence of N-CDs, indicating that N-CDs was a mixed type inhibitor [40–42]. In CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions,
E
c o r r
moved in a positive direction and the inhibition degree for cathodic reaction was lower than that for anodic reaction, implying that the anodic reaction was principally inhibited. However,
E
c o r r
moved in a negative direction with the increasing concentration of N-CDs in 1 M HCl solutions, demonstrating that the cathodic reaction was primarily restrained. Additionally, compared with the blank solution, the slope values of β a and β c change significantly with the addition of N-CDs. This was because the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the active sites on the metal surface hindered the effect on the dissolution mechanism of carbon steel. These results could be attributed to the fact that the N-CDs was adsorbed on the surface of N80 carbon steel, hindering the contact of solution/metal [41–43]. When the dosage was 200 mg/L, the η reached up to 93.2% and 95.4% in 1 M HCl and saline solution, respectively, indicating that the N-CDs was corrosion-resistant in two corrosive mediums.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) could also further reflect the inhibition performance. The Nyquist and Bode plots in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions with different N-CDs were shown in Fig. 8 . Clearly, it could be seen from the Nyquist diagram (Fig. 8(a, d)) that the impedance spectrum contained two capacitive loops, corresponding to double-layer capacitance and membrane resistance. The diameter of capacitive semicircle increased remarkably with the addition of various concentrations of N-CDs, this was assigned to the N80 steel surface being covered by N-CDs inhibitor molecules [28,32,34]. In other words, The density of the membrane was related to the concentration of the inhibitor N-CDs. Besides, the impedance modulus of |Z|0.01 Hz could also reflect the inhibition capability. Obviously, when the N80 steel was immersed into blank 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions for 72 h, the |Z|0.01 Hz value was 67.23
Ω
cm2 and 71.845
Ω
cm2 (Fig. 8(b, e)), respectively. This illustrated that the carbon steel was seriously corroded by saline and hydrochloric acid solutions. Conversely, as the inhibitor concentration of N-CDs increased, |Z|0.01 Hz value in both solutions significantly enhanced [8,35]. In addition, when the dosage was 200 mg/L, |Z|0.01 Hz presented the highest value among all conditions. These results indicated that N-CDs could exhibit excellent anti-corrosion performance to N80 carbon steel.

In order to further analyze the electrochemical process, the equivalent circuit (Fig. 9 was used to fit EIS impedance parameter. Where
R s
was the solution resistance,
R f
was the film resistance,
R
c t
was the charge transfer resistance and constant phase elements (CPE) was used instead of double layer capacitance to account for the heterogeneity caused by the roughness and impurities on the electrode surface.

The corrosion inhibition efficiency could be obtained by the following formula:
(4)
η =
R
c t
0
−
R
c t
R
c t
0
Where
R
c t
0
and
R
c t
were correspond to the charge transfer resistance with and without different concentrations of N-CDs. The impedance parameters obtained by fitting EIS data to equivalent circuits were shown in Table 2 . It was obvious that with the increase of the corrosion inhibitor concentration, the
R
c t
and
R f
values increased, indicating that the film formed by the corrosion inhibitor molecules on the surface of the N80 steel [44,45]. Nevertheless, The decrease of the
C P
E
d l
and
C P
E f
values was ascribed to the substitution of water molecules by N-CDs inhibitor molecules at the metal/solution interface and proved the protective effect of inhibitor molecules [46,47]. The EIS test results were consistent with dynamic polarization measurements and weight loss test results.

Fig. 10 revealed the corrosion morphology of N80 steel in different corrosion solutions. Obviously, the steel surface presented serious cracks and irregular grooves after 72 h immersion at 70 °C in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions (Fig. 10(a-d)). However, with the addition of 200 mg/L N-CDs inhibitor (as shown in Fig. 10(e-h)), the surface morphologies of N80 steel were relatively smooth compared with the blank solution, indicating that the damages of steel were greatly inhibited and cracks were alleviated in two aggressive solutions [48,49]. The results illustrated that N-CDs could effectively inhibit the corrosion of N80 steel. In addition, in the high-amplification SEM image, the surface of N80 carbon steel with N-CDs inhibitor in two aggressive solutions also had some irregular gullies, which indicated that when the concentration was certain, the corrosion of the N80 steel substrate reached equilibrium.

EDS images and corresponding elemental composition of N80 steel in the absence and presence of N-CDs in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions were presented in Fig. 11 . It could be seen from the EDS that the content of Fe in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions were 51.70% and 49.94% (Fig. 11(b) and (d)), respectively, but after adding 200 mg/L N-CDs corrosion inhibitor, the Fe content became 73.40% and 74.02% (Fig. 11(f) and (h)). The increases of Fe element demonstrated that the corrosion of N80 carbon steel was significantly alleviated [42,43]. Especially, in CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions, the content of C, O and Fe elements changed from 29.08%, 6.54%, 49.94% to 3.42%, 0.58% and 74.02% (Fig. 11(d) and (h)), respectively. Compared with the 1 M HCl solution, N-CDs was more anti-corrosion in CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions.

The AFM images of the N80 steel specimens immersed in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions with and without the concentration of 200 mg/L N-CDs for 72 h at 298 K were illustrated in Fig. 12 . The 2D and 3D AFM images (Fig. 12(a-d)) showed that the morphology of the N80 steel surface was strongly corroded by hydrochloric acid and brine solutions, but with the introduction of the N-CDs (Fig. 12(e-h)), the degree of surface corrosion was significantly alleviated. Furthermore, the average roughness (Ra) in hydrochloric and brine solutions decreased from 296 nm (corroded surface) to 129 nm and 213 nm to 98.8 nm by adding 200 mg/L N-CDs, respectively. These results illustrated that the introduction of corrosion inhibitors of N-CDs verified the protective effect.

The adsorption isotherm could be used to estimate the inhibition mechanism using the Langmuir isotherm model:
(5)
θ
1 − θ
=
K
a d s
C
where θ was the surface coverage, C was the concentration of corrosion inhibitor and Kads was the adsorption equilibrium constant. The relationship between C and C/θ values was displayed in Fig. 13 . It was observed that there was a good linear correlation between C and C/θ in the two different media solutions, and the goodness of fit (R2 value) of steel in HCl and NaCl was about 0.99101 and 0.9987, indicating that the adsorption process obeyed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The Kads values in HCl and NaCl were 24.75 and 47.73 L/g, respectively. The high Kads value indicated that the corrosion inhibitor molecules in the NaCl solution presented a strong adsorption capacity on the steel/solution interface. Moreover, the adsorption free energy (
Δ
G
a d s
0
) was used to evaluate the type of adsorption:
(6)
Δ
G
a d s
0
= − R T L n
( 1000
K
a d s
)
where R was the molar gas constant and T was the absolute temperature (343 K), respectively. When
Δ
G
a d s
0
value was > -20 kJ/mol, it was generally considered that there was electrostatic interaction between the corrosion inhibitor molecule and the metal surface, which was called physical adsorption; when the value was <-40 kJ/mol, the adsorption type was generally considered to be chemical adsorption. the Kads values of steel in HCl and NaCl solutions were -28.859 and -30.712 kJ/mol, respectively, manifesting that the interaction between N-CDs and N80 steel surface involved chemical adsorption and physical adsorption in both solutions.

Through the analysis of the structural components of N-CDs, it was found that N-CDs were spherical particles with a particle diameter of 10–24 nm, with abundant functional groups (hydroxyl, amino, double bonds, etc.) attached to the surface. The functional group containing a lone pair of electrons (such as N-O, C=O, N-C=O, N-H, etc.) was combined with the empty d orbital of Fe2+ and was firmly adsorbed on the surface of carbon steel. At the same time, in the corrosive medium, the surface of the carbon steel would accumulate charge, and those functional groups could interact with the charge to enhance its interaction. The electrochemical results and the surface morphology analysis of carbon steel also further proved that the nanoparticles of N-CDs could effectively adhere to the steel surface through diffusion and clusters to form a similar film to inhibit the corrosion of N80 steel [21,23]. The related literature was consulted in the early stage, and the corrosion inhibition mechanism of N-CDs in hydrochloric acid and salt solution was established. The process of N-CDs inhibiting carbon steel could be explained from the following aspects (Fig. 14 (a, b)). In the blank HCl solution, the corrosion behavior of N80 steel mainly presented the dissolution of Fe at the anode and the hydrogen evolution reaction at the cathode (Fig. 14(a)). When N-CDs were added, the inhibitor's inhibitory behavior on N80 steel showed that the functional groups containing lone pairs of electrons (N, O) on the surface of N-CDs could coordinate with the empty d orbitals of Fe2+, which promoted some nanoparticles N-CDs to be adsorbed on the metal surface through chemical interaction. In addition, due to the abundant functional groups on the surface of N-CDs, the particles would be attracted by electrostatic attraction to form agglomerated nanoparticles, and then diffused to the surface of the carbon steel to form a hydrophobic protective layer, which ultimately hindered the reaction of the N80 steel anode and cathode [22,25,26,33]. The hydrophobic protective film could effectively inhibit the contact between carbon steel and corrosive media. In saline solution, the corrosion of N80 carbon steel was mainly manifested by the dissolution of anode Fe and the oxygen absorption corrosion of cathode to produce Fe(OH)2. During this process, Fe(OH)2 was gradually oxidized to Fe2O3•xH2O (Fig 14(b)). When N-CDs were added, the inhibition behavior first manifested as a layer of Fe2O3•xH2O formed on the surface of N80 steel. Secondly, N-CDs gradually adhered to the surface of N80 carbon steel through physical and chemical adsorption to form a corrosion inhibitor film. Therefore, the saltwater corrosion inhibition mechanism was not only the physical and chemical adsorption of N-CDs particles but also the formation of a layer of iron-containing oxides on the surface of the carbon steel to isolate corrosive ions (such as Cl−) from contacting the carbon steel.

In this study, the structure and corrosion properties of the synthesized N-CDs in 1 M HCl and CO2 saturated 3.5 wt % NaCl solution were evaluated. The characterization analysis of the synthesized N-CDs showed that the surface of the prepared N-CDs contained abundant functional groups, these characteristics were conducive to the adsorption of nanomaterials on the N80 carbon steel surface. Corrosion inhibition behavior results indicated that N-CDs could alleviate the corrosion of N80 steel in aggressive solution, the inhibition efficiency reached up to 89.04% (1 M HCl) and 94.6% (3.5 wt % NaCl), respectively. The electrochemical results further revealed that N-CDs was a mixed corrosion inhibitor in the two corrosive conditions, and functional groups could be adsorbed on the N80 carbon steel to prevent the contact between the metal and the corrosive solution. Furthermore, morphology analysis and observation results revealed that the addition of inhibitors could effectively inhibit surface pits and cracks and further verified that the adsorption of N-CDs on the surface of N80 steel obeyed Langmuir model. More importantly, as a nanomaterial, N-CDs showed certain corrosion resistance to carbon steel, which provided new options for the corrosion industry.

Xiaodan Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jianbo Li: Project administration. Chaoyi Deng: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Lan Yang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Jie Lv: Visualization. Luoping Fu: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We would like to submit our manuscript entitle “Novel carbon dots as effective corrosion inhibitor for N80 steel in 1 M HCl and CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions” to “Journal of molecular structure”.

No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part. All the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Southwest Petroleum University, Institute, Chengdu, China and the Scientific Compass Test Platform for providing the research facilities needed for the above study.
